chapters.title: Being a good person is a full time job
chapters.text:
On paper, being good is simple. You say thank you a lot, help older women cross the street, and pick vegetables over meat. Things get messy the moment theory meets real life. The woman you helped turns out to hold awful views, and the vegetables you bought were shipped from far away with a giant carbon footprint. The clothes you wore that day were made in a factory that uses child labor. When the heavenly accountant tallies your good deeds at the end of the day, your score is almost negative despite your best efforts.

Our world is so tangled that being good can feel impossible. So the question becomes, should we even try. To answer it and many others, Michael Schur walks us through the long history of moral philosophy. The greatest minds have wrestled with these questions for thousands of years, and surely they have something to say.

Ethics and moral philosophy can feel distant at first, like a room full of bearded old men and very thick books. Soon, though, you see how deeply they connect to daily life and how practical they can be.

Almost everything we say or do has at least some ethical layer, even when we miss it.

Let us jump in and look at the three main Western schools of thought, virtue ethics, deontology, and utilitarianism. Each has something valuable to offer, so let us pull out the best pieces.

chapters.title: Aristotle is still relevant more than two thousand years later
chapters.text:
Today you have decided to become a good person. Great. But how do we tell good from bad in a person. It is much harder than it sounds, and a long line of moral philosophers is here to help. The three major schools of thought are where the real work happens.

The oldest and most traditional of the three, virtue ethics, tackles the question of what makes someone good or bad. Aristotle, the father of virtue ethics, built a system to guide us. He said a person is good when they hold specific qualities, the virtues, in the right amount. Let us unpack that.

To get a fuller picture, Aristotle focuses on a few questions. Which qualities should a good person have. In what amounts. Can anyone acquire them. How do we acquire them. How do we feel when we have them.

He starts with the last one and points to a person's deepest purpose. Aristotle puts the goal into a single word, eudaimonia, which roughly means happiness or flourishing. Flourishing fits best when we talk about the point of being alive, since it points to total fulfillment and wholeness. To reach that state, we first need the virtues.

Aristotle describes virtues as qualities that allow those who have them to be in a good state and to carry out their functions well. The virtue of a cake might be deliciousness, which serves its core purpose of satisfying appetite. In the same way, human virtues like generosity, courage, and integrity serve our deeper purpose, which is flourishing.

None of us are born with a full set of virtues, but we all have the potential to build them. Each virtue is a skill that takes practice and steady work.

Virtues are built through habit. A person becomes honest by acting honestly, and just by acting justly.

Aristotle also points out that we need the right amount of each virtue, not too much and not too little. Be kind, but not so kind that people walk all over you. Be brave, but not so brave that you run into every burning building you see.

chapters.title: Flourishing needs balance
chapters.text:
The golden mean is one of Aristotle's most valuable ideas. It shows that balance matters in everything, even the harder qualities or emotions. We could not respond to cruel and unjust acts without a sense of anger. To become good people, we have to turn each virtue into a balanced scale.

The hard part is that the perfect middle is almost impossible to describe or measure. How angry is too angry. How brave is too brave. Aristotle himself wrestled with these questions. Keep exploring the edges of virtues and watching how they show up in your own actions and the actions of others, and you will start to sense the right balance when you see it.

The search for golden means builds on itself, and each new one gets easier to find.

That is the beauty of virtue ethics. It helps you understand yourself and others, and it gives you a practical guide to being a better person. Aristotle's ideal is also within reach. He promises that if you keep searching, learning, and practicing the virtues, you are guaranteed to become a more adaptable, proactive, curious, and considerate person. You will truly flourish.

Try again, fail again, fail better, as Michael Schur puts it.

With all this talk of virtues, we should also talk about vices, especially one that the philosopher Judith Shklar calls the worst of all, cruelty. Unlike the traditional deadly sins, which break divine rules, cruelty is, in a strange twist, uniquely human. We have to put it at the top of the worst crimes list and avoid it at all costs. Cruelty is rarely justified, and it almost always goes past whatever caused it. It is the only quality that does not need Aristotelian balance.

Life will throw many subtle ethical questions at us, and virtue ethics, along with other philosophies, can serve as a guiding light. Being a good person is hard, and the right theoretical map makes it more manageable.

chapters.title: Utilitarianism trades fog for clear numbers
chapters.text:
Stop two on our tour is utilitarianism. Two British thinkers of the 19th century, Jeremy Bentham and John Stuart Mill, gave the doctrine its shape. It cares about outcomes, not the deeds themselves and not the character of the doer.

For doctrine to matter, the wants of every human have to count the same.

Once you take the utilitarian view, knotty questions get simpler. The correct path is whichever route lifts the most people into joy. That has real charm, since you can sense whether you helped or harmed, and the data can be checked.

Ethics stops feeling gauzy and starts feeling like arithmetic. Bentham even handed us a yardstick. He drew up seven rulers to weigh the good that any choice produces.

Intensity, or how deep the gladness runs. Duration, or how long it lingers. Certainty, or how firm the upside is. Propinquity, or how near in time. Fecundity, or how long the ripple keeps moving. Purity, or the balance of hurt against joy. Extent, or how many souls reap the gain.

Bentham tried his best, but let us face it, this kit is clunky in the real world. Picture running those sums before every snack you grab.

Utilitarianism can stumble in odd corners. In theory, you could butcher one well person to farm their organs and rescue eight sick strangers. The ledger says eight saved outweighs one lost, yet the gut rebels.

Even with those rough patches, the school is resurging. Its fingerprints are on every policy desk. A fresh sample is the rollout of Covid 19 shots. Governments around the globe pushed workers in critical jobs and the most fragile citizens to the front of the line, squeezing the largest public good out of every vial. That sequence is textbook utilitarianism.

The lesson is caution. No single doctrine owns the answer. Borrow from each as the moment calls, so you can lift the strongest piece from each school.

Besides his pleasure gauge, Bentham minted new units. Hedons would track joy, and dolors would track suffering.

chapters.title: Kant turns the screws even tighter
chapters.text:
The third and final stop belongs to deontology and the worldview of Immanuel Kant.

Deontology is the branch of ethics that lives and dies by duty. Kant, a top voice in the camp, argued that we have to build a tight set of rules with our reason and then obey them in every tight spot. The books are tougher to chew than Aristotle or Mill, but the headline, the categorical imperative, is short. Act only on the rule that you could will to become a law for everyone.

In plainer speech, the imperative asks you to find rules that survive being used by anybody, not just you. Before you act, picture the whole world doing the same thing. If that world would still be livable, the act passes. A Kantian would call lying to a pal a no, because a planet of liars would be chaos. Under Kant, the lie is barred, even when a killer promises to harm your dog if you refuse.

Kant refuses wiggle room. An act carries moral worth only when it follows a maxim, a rule carved out by pure reason. Other motives do not register as moral for him, even when the result lines up. Donating from pity rates below donating because you have decided it is the only rule worth keeping and you made it your maxim.

Kant holds that the only path to moral action runs through duty.

Trying to live by this doctrine is almost always a head game. It is plain in shape, but heavy on the soul. There is also a softer form of Kant's rule, friendlier to real people. It reads, treat humanity, in your own person or in any other, always as an end, never as a means alone.

Do the best you can until you know better. Then, when you know better, do better, as Maya Angelou has said.

chapters.title: We all live inside a quiet social contract
chapters.text:
Past the great columns of philosophy stands one more book that earns a seat on this tour. It does not grab headlines, yet it is a clean map for becoming a better human, T. M. Scanlon's What We Owe to Each Other.

Scanlon's framework, contractualism, runs on the idea that people will reshape their personal wants when the bending serves the group. Under the framework, each person treats the concerns of others as equal to their own. Selfish moves get pushed aside, and trades open up where both sides walk away with something.

Scanlon's twist is to hand every person a veto over any rule before it gets locked in. Unfair rules get shot down, so the survivors are the ones most people can accept. Rules that fail the test never see daylight. The end result is a set of norms that fit everyone and serve everyone's needs.

In a tangled modern world, spotting selfish and unfair moves fast is the seed of trust and peace.

Scanlon's project is more down to earth than the others we have walked through. He trusts that people can reason with each other and close the gaps. Since most of our hours are spent with the people right next to us, it makes sense to draw the moral lines of our small worlds together with them.

Scanlon's rules are a moral floor, the least a just society owes itself. If you want more than a passing grade in life, you have to reach higher. Use contractualism to strip away the worst behavior, then climb higher by making service to others your new north star.

The pandemic threw contractualism into sharp relief. Mask wearing became a compact we all signed to guard each other and meet shared needs. Yet millions shrugged off even that modest ask and put their own comforts first. What we owed each other in those months was small, but the return was enormous. A small civic gesture, refused by many, could have spared countless hospital beds and family goodbyes. That refusal is the lesson Scanlon would want us to sit with, and the price we paid for it lives in our collective memory.

chapters.title: How to use these ideas in real life
chapters.text:
So how do we become perfect. The hard truth is that we cannot. We can become good people, which is, in some ways, even better. Reaching this point in the summary is already a big step toward that goal, since you have made a clear choice to change. You also care and you are willing to learn, which is almost half the work.

On the bumpy road through moral philosophy, a few things are worth keeping in mind. Not everything has to be about morality, so do not obsess over theories and rules. They are guidelines, not commandments. We do not have to be perfect to be good, since moral perfection is out of reach. A person is a person through others, so make helping and respecting others part of your purpose. Only a sincere apology can heal a moral wrong, and being a good person means knowing how and when to apologize.

The journey toward being a good person is unpredictable and tiring. You may fail more times than you can count, and each failure is a step in the right direction and a useful lesson. Hold onto that truth as you go.

Try this

Watch Michael Schur's series The Good Place to see how he turns the philosophical ideas from this summary into action. Think about the theories from the key points above and pick the parts you want to bring into your life.
